level_2.origins_history:
Aglianico is an ancient grape from southern Italy, despite centuries of mistaken belief in Greek origins. The earliest written mention appeared in 1520 in an archive document recording that Giulio Antonio Acquaviva d'Aragona, Count of Conversano, owned fields planted with grapevines called Aglianiche.

The supposed Greek etymology linking Aglianico to 'hellanico' (hellenic) dates to 1581, when Neapolitan scholar Giambattista della Porta tentatively identified Pliny's Helvola grape with a hypothetical Greek variety. However, this etymology has been thoroughly debunked: Helvola was a yellowish wine while Aglianico has black berries; linguists confirm that 'aglianico' cannot derive from 'ellenico'; and Roman authors always used 'graecus' rather than 'hellenicus' to indicate Greek origin.

The name most likely derives from the Spanish 'llano' (plain), since Spain occupied central Italy during the 15th and 16th centuries. The Spanish 'lla' is pronounced 'glia' in Italian, making Aglianico 'the grape of the plain,' reflected in its old dialect names A Glianica and La Glianica. DNA profiling confirms Aglianico has no close relationship to any modern Greek variety.

genetic_lineage.overview: Aglianico is an ancient variety with no confirmed parents, though DNA analysis has established it as distinctly southern Italian rather than Greek in origin. It is genetically related to several Campanian and Basilicatan varieties and may be a parent of Aglianicone.

parents.name: Unknown
parents.notes: No parents have been identified through DNA analysis. The variety appears to be an ancient indigenous grape of southern Italy.

offspring.name: Aglianicone
offspring.notes: A high-yielding, low-quality Campanian variety. DNA paternity testing suggests Aglianico could be a parent. Cultivated in Campania and Basilicata with about 140 ha planted.

biotypes.name: Taurasi
biotypes.description: Small berries, less vigorous growth, cylindrical clusters with slight tendency to millerandage. Produces structured, tannic wines requiring extended aging.
biotypes.wine_style: Most austere and age-worthy style, firm tannins, darker fruit

biotypes.name: Taburno
biotypes.description: Largest bunches of the three main biotypes, high natural acidity. Named for the Taburno mountain area in Benevento province.
biotypes.wine_style: High acidity, good structure, medium body

biotypes.name: Vulture
biotypes.description: Medium-sized berries, slightly larger than Taurasi biotype. Renders wines that are more accessible at a younger age due to higher skin-to-pulp ratio.
biotypes.wine_style: More fruit-driven and elegantly nuanced, accessible younger than Taurasi

genetic_lineage.genetic_significance: Despite centuries of attribution to Greek origins, DNA profiling has definitively shown Aglianico has no relationship to modern Greek varieties. The genetic evidence, combined with linguistic analysis debunking the Hellenistic etymology, confirms Aglianico as an ancient indigenous variety of southern Italy, probably originating in Campania or Basilicata.

clones.name: Clone 2
clones.characteristics: Taurasi biotype. Excellent structure but less aromatic and polyphenolic intensity than other clones. Best blended with Clone 7 and Clone 13 for balanced wines.

clones.name: Clone 7
clones.characteristics: Taurasi biotype. Moderate aromatic and polyphenolic qualities with soft tannins. Suited for medium aging potential wines.

clones.name: Clone 13
clones.characteristics: Taurasi biotype. Very positive polyphenolic framework with excellent anthocyanin content. Floral notes. Recommended for extended aging when blended with Clone 7 and Clone 2.

clones.name: Clone 23
clones.characteristics: Taurasi biotype. Intense purplish-red color with violet and berry scents that are persistent and delicate. Suitable for long aging.

clones.name: Clone 103
clones.characteristics: Characteristics between Vulture and Taurasi types. Excellent anthocyanin levels and balanced aromatics. Versatile clone.

clones.name: Clone 106
clones.characteristics: Aromatic profile with intense floral and fruity scents. Suited for young wines, possibly blended with Piedirosso.

clones.name: Clone 109
clones.characteristics: Ideal for young, fruity-floral wines. Particularly soft tannins make it accessible early.

clones.name: Clone 111
clones.characteristics: Strong anthocyanin content, structure and aromatic components. Intense hints of ripe fruit with phenol-leather notes. Suitable for extended aging.

clones.name: Clone 421
clones.characteristics: Taurasi biotype. Broad aromatic range with notes of strawberry, cocoa, and licorice. Suited for medium-to-long aging or young consumption.

clones.name: Clone 381
clones.characteristics: Taburno biotype. Floral and fruity aromas with pronounced spice including black pepper and cloves. Full-bodied with sweet, slightly bitter tannins. Medium-to-extended aging potential.

clones.name: Clone 11
clones.characteristics: Aglianico del Vulture biotype. Very interesting aromatic profile, especially glycosidic components. Fairly good polyphenolic framework. Recommended for prolonged aging.

clones.name: Clone 14
clones.characteristics: Aglianico del Vulture biotype. Positive polyphenolic framework with clear floral and fruity notes. Ideal when blended with Clone 11 or Taurasi types Clone 2 and Clone 23.

terroir.effect: The signature soil of both Taurasi and Vulture. Volcanic soils provide mineral richness, excellent drainage, and temperature regulation as volcanic rocks retain heat. Vines face stress that leads to concentrated flavors. The volcanic origin adds a distinctive smokiness to wines.

wine_character.aromatics: Smoke, ash, dark fruit, chocolate, tar

terroir.effect: Found in parts of both Campania and Basilicata. Provides good drainage while retaining some moisture. Contributes to wine freshness and minerality.
More common in lower elevation sites. Water-retentive soils produce slightly softer wines with broader structure.

susceptibilities.notes: Despite thick skins, the very late ripening (sometimes into November) exposes clusters to autumn rains and humidity, making botrytis a significant concern.
susceptibilities.impact_on_wine: Reduces quality, can cause off-flavors if affected fruit is not sorted

resistances.notes: Thick skins provide good natural resistance to powdery mildew (oidium).

disease_profile.general_notes: Aglianico buds early, making it vulnerable to spring frosts, but ripens very late, sometimes not until November. This long growing season is essential to tame its naturally high tannins. The variety is vigorous and yields need to be controlled for quality. Thrives at 200-600m elevation in cool but dry, sunny mountainous conditions.

regional_expressions.style_summary: The most prestigious Aglianico appellation, elevated to DOCG in 1993. High-altitude vineyards (400-500m) in the shadow of Vesuvius produce Italy's most structured, age-worthy Aglianico. Often called the 'Barolo of the South' for its tannic power and longevity. Minimum 85% Aglianico, Riserva requires 3 years aging.

typical_character.aromatics: Dark plum, chocolate, tar, smoke, leather, dried herbs

regional_expressions.style_summary: From the slopes of extinct Monte Vulture volcano in Basilicata. Elevated to DOCG (Superiore) in 2010. Must be 100% Aglianico. More accessible at a younger age than Taurasi due to slightly larger berries and higher fruit-to-skin ratio. Still substantial wines with good aging potential.

typical_character.aromatics: Red and black fruit, violets, mineral, smoke

regional_expressions.style_summary: From the Taburno mountain area in Benevento province, Campania. The Taburno biotype is known for high natural acidity and the largest bunches. Wines are structured with good acidity but generally more approachable than Taurasi.

Aglianico appears in numerous other Campanian appellations including Falerno del Massico, Irpinia, and Lacryma Christi del Vesuvio (as a minor blending component). These wines are generally more accessible and earlier-drinking than the DOCG wines.

A handful of Australian producers concerned by climate change have embraced Aglianico. Westend Estate in Griffith, New South Wales (owned by the Calabria family, descended from Italian immigrants) has been particularly successful, winning Best Red Wine at the 2010 Australian Alternative Varieties Wine Show.
Small plantings exist in California, where producers make more rounded, accessible versions than Italian originals. Kenneth Volk in Paso Robles is a noted producer of an Italianate but softer style.
